narrative: And now, assuming children of both sexes to have been born, it will be proper for us to consider, in the next place, their nurture and education; this cannot be left altogether unnoticed, and yet may be thought a subject fitted rather for precept and admonition than for law. In private life there are many little things, not always apparent, arising out of the pleasures and pains and desires of individuals, which run counter to the intention of the legislator, and make the characters of the citizens various and dissimilar -- this is an evil in states; for by reason of their smallness and frequent occurrence, there would be an unseemliness and want of propriety in making them penal by law; and if made penal, they are the destruction of the written law because mankind get the habit of frequently transgressing the law in small matters. The result is that you cannot legislate about them, and still less can you be silent. I speak somewhat darkly, but I shall endeavour also to bring my wares into the light of day, for I acknowledge that at present there is a want of clearness in what I am saying.
refashioned: Now that we have assumed children of both sexes have been born, we should next consider their upbringing and education. This subject cannot be ignored entirely, yet it may seem better suited to guidance and advice than to formal legislation. In private life, there are countless small matters -- not always visible -- arising from individual pleasures, pains, and desires, that work against the legislator's intentions and produce citizens of widely varying character. This is a real problem for states. Because these matters are so trivial individually yet so frequent, it would be improper and undignified to make them criminal offenses. Yet if you do penalize them, you destroy the authority of written law itself, because people develop the habit of breaking the law in minor ways. The result is a dilemma: you cannot legislate about these things, but neither can you remain silent about them. I realize I am speaking rather obscurely, but I will try to bring my meaning into the open, since I admit that what I am saying lacks clarity at present.
elegant: With children now born, we must address their nurture and education -- a subject better suited to guidance than rigid law. Private life is full of small matters arising from individual pleasures and pains that quietly undermine the legislator's aims, producing inconsistent citizens. Making such trivialities criminal is undignified; yet penalizing them erodes respect for all law, since people learn to transgress habitually in small things. You cannot legislate these matters, but you cannot ignore them either. I speak obscurely now, but will clarify.
summary: The Athenian introduces the topic of children's education, noting the paradox that many private habits shaping character are too trivial to legislate yet too important to ignore. Penalizing small matters erodes respect for law, but silence allows bad habits to flourish.
distilled: Many habits that shape character are too small to legislate but too important to ignore, creating a fundamental dilemma for lawgivers.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger (monologue). This passage introduces Book 7's central theme of education. The distinction between formal law and informal custom echoes Republic 425a-427a, where Socrates argues against legislating petty matters. The concept of 'unwritten laws' (agrapha nomima) was important in Greek political thought -- compare Thucydides 2.37. The Athenian's admission of speaking 'darkly' is characteristic of the Laws' self-aware methodology.

narrative:
"Very true."

"Am I not right in maintaining that a good education is that which tends most to the improvement of mind and body?"

"Undoubtedly."

"And nothing can be plainer than that the fairest bodies are those which grow up from infancy in the best and straightest manner?"

"Certainly."

"And do we not further observe that the first shoot of every living thing is by far the greatest and fullest? Many will even contend that a man at twenty-five does not reach twice the height which he attained at five."

"True."

"Well, and is not rapid growth without proper and abundant exercise the source of endless evils in the body?"

"Yes."

"And the body should have the most exercise when it receives most nourishment?"

"But, Stranger, are we to impose this great amount of exercise upon newly-born infants?"

"Nay, rather on the bodies of infants still unborn."

"What do you mean, my good sir? In the process of gestation?"

"Exactly. I am not at all surprised that you have never heard of this very peculiar sort of gymnastic applied to such little creatures, which, although strange, I will endeavour to explain to you."

"By all means."
refashioned:
Cleinias agreed. The Athenian then asked whether good education is that which best improves both mind and body. Cleinias confirmed this without hesitation. The Athenian continued: surely the finest bodies are those that grow from infancy in the healthiest, most natural way. And the first growth spurt of every living thing is by far the greatest -- many would argue that a person at twenty-five has not even doubled the height they reached at five. Rapid growth without sufficient exercise is the source of countless physical problems. The body needs the most exercise precisely when it receives the most nourishment.

Cleinias objected: are we really going to demand heavy exercise from newborn infants? The Athenian surprised him -- not newborns, but the unborn. Cleinias was astonished: exercise during pregnancy? Exactly, the Athenian replied. He was not surprised Cleinias had never heard of this unusual form of prenatal gymnastics, but he would do his best to explain it. Cleinias urged him to proceed.
elegant: Good education improves both mind and body. The finest bodies develop from proper care beginning in infancy. Early growth is the most dramatic -- a person at twenty-five may not have doubled their height at five. Rapid growth without exercise causes great harm, and the body needs the most movement when it receives the most nourishment. This principle applies not just to infants but to the unborn -- prenatal exercise matters.
summary: The Athenian argues that education must develop both mind and body, and that the earliest growth period is the most critical. He shocks Cleinias by extending this principle to prenatal care, arguing that exercise during pregnancy benefits the unborn child.
distilled: The earliest period of growth is the most critical, and the principle that the body needs exercise proportional to its nourishment applies even before birth.
notes: Speakers: Cleinias and Athenian Stranger (dialogue). The principle that education encompasses both body and soul echoes Republic 376e, where Socrates proposes gymnastic for the body and music for the soul. The emphasis on early childhood development is remarkably modern. The prenatal exercise idea, while unusual, connects to the Greek medical tradition (Hippocratic corpus) and anticipates the detailed physical regimen of Book 7. Compare also Aristotle's Politics 7.16, which directly draws on this passage for its own prenatal recommendations.

narrative:
"The practice is more easy for us to understand than for you, by reason of certain amusements which are carried to excess by us at Athens. Not only boys, but often older persons, are in the habit of keeping quails and cocks, which they train to fight one another. And they are far from thinking that the contests in which they stir them up to fight with one another are sufficient exercise; for, in addition to this, they carry them about tucked beneath their armpits, holding the smaller birds in their hands, the larger under their arms, and go for a walk of a great many miles for the sake of health, that is to say, not their own health, but the health of the birds; whereby they prove to any intelligent person, that all bodies are benefited by shakings and movements, when they are moved without weariness, whether motion proceeds from themselves, or is caused by a swing, or at sea, or on horseback, or by other bodies in whatever way moving, and that thus gaining the mastery over food and drink, they are able to impart beauty and health and strength.

"But admitting all this, what follows? Shall we make a ridiculous law that the pregnant woman shall walk about and fashion the embryo within as we fashion wax before it hardens, and after birth swathe the infant for two years? Suppose that we compel nurses, under penalty of a legal fine, to be always carrying the children somewhere or other, either to the temples, or into the country, or to their relations' houses, until they are well able to stand, and to take care that their limbs are not distorted by leaning on them when they are too young -- they should continue to carry them until the infant has completed its third year; the nurses should be strong, and there should be more than one of them. Shall these be our rules, and shall we impose a penalty for the neglect of them? No, no; the penalty of which we were speaking will fall upon our own heads more than enough."
refashioned:
The Athenian explained that this concept was easier for Athenians to grasp because of a common practice taken to extremes in Athens. Not only boys but also older men kept quails and fighting cocks, training them to battle each other. But they did not consider the fights alone to be enough exercise for the birds. In addition, they carried them around -- the smaller birds in their hands, the larger ones tucked under their arms -- walking many miles not for their own health, but for the health of the birds. This proved to any thoughtful observer that all bodies benefit from being shaken and moved, provided the motion does not cause exhaustion. It does not matter whether the movement comes from the creature itself or from an external source -- a swing, the sea, horseback, or any other moving body. Through such motion, bodies master their food and drink and gain beauty, health, and strength.

But what follows from this? Should they pass a ridiculous law requiring pregnant women to walk about, shaping the embryo within like wax before it hardens? And after birth, should infants be swaddled for two years? Should they compel nurses, under threat of fines, to carry children everywhere -- to temples, to the countryside, to relatives' homes -- until the children can stand on their own, ensuring their limbs are not warped from bearing weight too early? The nurses should be strong, and there should be more than one per child. Should they carry them until the child turns three? Should they impose penalties for neglecting these rules? No -- the penalty they spoke of earlier would fall on their own heads quite enough already.
elegant: Athenians understand prenatal motion because they carry fighting birds for miles -- not for their own health but for the birds'. This proves all bodies benefit from movement that does not exhaust them, whether self-generated or external. But should we therefore legislate that pregnant women must walk to shape their unborn children like wax? That nurses must carry infants everywhere until age three, under penalty of fines? No -- attempting to enforce such rules would bring more ridicule upon ourselves than compliance from others.
summary: The Athenian uses the Athenian practice of carrying fighting birds for exercise as proof that all bodies benefit from gentle motion. He then proposes -- and immediately retracts -- the idea of legally mandating prenatal walks and infant-carrying, acknowledging such laws would be ridiculous to enforce.
distilled: While gentle motion benefits all bodies including the unborn, attempting to enforce such care through law would invite ridicule rather than compliance.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger (monologue). The quail and cock-fighting reference reveals everyday Athenian life -- bird-fighting was a popular pastime (see Aristophanes' Birds; Aelian, De Natura Animalium). The analogy between shaping wax and shaping embryos connects to Greek embryological theories. The self-deprecating withdrawal of the proposed law ('the penalty will fall on our own heads') demonstrates the Athenian's awareness that legislation has practical limits -- a theme running through Book 7. The passage anticipates the later distinction between law and custom (7.12).

narrative:
"What penalty?"

"Ridicule, and the difficulty of getting the feminine and servant-like dispositions of the nurses to comply."

"Then why was there any need to speak of the matter at all?"

"The reason is that masters and freemen in states, when they hear of it, are very likely to arrive at a true conviction that without due regulation of private life in cities, stability in the laying down of laws is hardly to be expected; and he who makes this reflection may himself adopt the laws just now mentioned, and, adopting them, may order his house and state well and be happy."

"Likely enough."

"And therefore let us proceed with our legislation until we have determined the exercises which are suited to the souls of young children, in the same manner in which we have begun to go through the rules relating to their bodies."

"By all means."
refashioned:
Cleinias asked what penalty. The Athenian replied: ridicule, and the near impossibility of getting nurses -- with their subservient and resistant dispositions -- to comply. Cleinias then asked why bother raising the subject at all.

The Athenian explained: because when masters and free citizens in states hear such reasoning, they are very likely to reach the conviction that without proper regulation of private life, stability in public law is hardly achievable. A person who grasps this truth may voluntarily adopt the practices just described, and by doing so, order both household and state well, and be happy.

Cleinias conceded this was likely enough. The Athenian proposed they continue their legislation, now turning to exercises suited to the souls of young children, just as they had been addressing rules for their bodies. Cleinias agreed.
elegant: The penalty for such laws would be ridicule and non-compliance from nurses. But the discussion has value: when thoughtful citizens hear the reasoning, they may voluntarily adopt these practices, understanding that stable public law depends on well-ordered private life. Let us now turn from bodily exercises to those suited to children's souls.
summary: The Athenian explains that while such rules cannot be enforced as laws, discussing them can persuade thoughtful citizens to adopt good practices voluntarily. He then proposes moving from physical to psychological education of young children.
distilled: Though unenforceable as law, sound principles of child-rearing can be voluntarily adopted by thoughtful citizens who understand that public order depends on private discipline.
notes: Speakers: Cleinias and Athenian Stranger (dialogue). The remark about 'feminine and servant-like dispositions' reflects the patriarchal assumptions of ancient Athens -- though the Laws is notably more progressive on women's roles than most Greek texts (see 7.39, 7.42). The key philosophical point -- that voluntary adoption of good practices matters more than legal compulsion -- connects to the concept of the 'prelude' (prooimion) developed in Laws 4.722d-723d. The transition from body to soul mirrors the Republic's dual education in gymnastic and music (Republic 376e).

narrative:
"Let us assume, then, as a first principle in relation both to the body and soul of very young creatures, that nursing and moving about by day and night is good for them all, and that the younger they are, the more they will need it; infants should live, if that were possible, as if they were always rocking at sea. This is the lesson which we may gather from the experience of nurses, and likewise from the use of the remedy of motion in the rites of the Corybantes; for when mothers want their restless children to go to sleep they do not employ rest, but, on the contrary, motion -- rocking them in their arms; nor do they give them silence, but they sing to them and lap them in sweet strains; and the Bacchic women are cured of their frenzy in the same manner by the use of the dance and of music."

"Well, Stranger, and what is the reason of this?"

"The reason is obvious."

"What?"
refashioned:
The Athenian established a first principle for both the body and soul of very young children: movement and nursing by day and night is beneficial for all of them, and the younger they are, the more they need it. Ideally, infants should live as though constantly rocking at sea.

This lesson comes from the experience of nurses and from the healing use of motion in the rites of the Corybantes. When mothers want their restless children to sleep, they do not use stillness but the opposite -- movement, rocking them in their arms. They do not use silence but song, wrapping their children in gentle melodies. The Bacchic women are cured of their frenzy in exactly the same way, through dance and music.

Cleinias asked what the reason for this could be. The Athenian replied that the reason was obvious. Cleinias pressed him to explain.
elegant: First principle: constant gentle motion is good for the bodies and souls of young children -- the younger, the more they need it. Ideally, infants would live as if always rocking at sea. Nurses know this instinctively: to calm restless children, they use movement, not stillness; song, not silence. The Bacchic women are cured of frenzy through the same remedy -- dance and music.
summary: The Athenian states a first principle: gentle constant motion benefits both body and soul in the very young. He supports this with the evidence of nurses who rock and sing to calm children, and Bacchic rites that cure frenzy through dance and music.
distilled: Constant gentle motion and music calm and benefit infants' bodies and souls, as shown by the universal practice of rocking and singing to children.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias (dialogue). The Corybantes were ecstatic priests of Cybele whose rites involved wild dancing and music believed to have healing properties -- referenced also in Symposium 215e and Ion 533e-534a. The Bacchic women (Maenads) experienced divine frenzy in Dionysian rites. The parallel between infant care and religious ecstasy is a striking psychological insight -- both involve using controlled external motion to override internal agitation. This anticipates the explicit therapeutic theory in the next passage (7.6).

narrative: "The affection both of the Bacchantes and of the children is an emotion of fear, which springs out of an evil habit of the soul. And when some one applies external agitation to affections of this sort, the motion coming from without gets the better of the terrible and violent internal one, and produces a peace and calm in the soul, and quiets the restless palpitation of the heart, which is a thing much to be desired, sending the children to sleep, and making the Bacchantes, although they remain awake, to dance to the pipe with the help of the Gods to whom they offer acceptable sacrifices, and producing in them a sound mind, which takes the place of their frenzy. And, to express what I mean in a word, there is a good deal to be said in favour of this treatment."
refashioned: The Athenian explained that the condition of both the Bacchantes and restless children is fundamentally an emotion of fear, arising from a disordered state of the soul. When someone applies external movement to this kind of agitation, the external motion overpowers the terrible and violent internal one. It produces peace and calm in the soul and quiets the restless pounding of the heart -- a highly desirable result. In children, this external motion sends them to sleep. In the Bacchantes, who remain awake, it replaces their frenzy with orderly dancing to the pipe, accompanied by the gods to whom they offer pleasing sacrifices, and produces a sound mind in place of madness. In short, there is much to be said in favor of this treatment.
elegant: Both the Bacchantes' frenzy and children's restlessness are forms of fear rooted in a disordered soul. When external motion is applied, it overpowers the violent internal agitation, producing calm. Children fall asleep; Bacchantes replace frenzy with orderly dance. External rhythm masters internal chaos -- there is much to commend this treatment.
summary: The Athenian explains that both infant restlessness and Bacchic frenzy stem from fear and inner disorder. External rhythmic motion overpowers the violent internal agitation, producing calm -- sleep in children, sound-minded dance in the Bacchantes.
distilled: External rhythmic motion overpowers internal agitation by replacing disordered fear with calm, whether putting children to sleep or transforming Bacchic frenzy into orderly dance.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger (monologue). This passage presents a remarkable proto-psychological theory: anxiety and restlessness are forms of fear caused by internal disorder, and external rhythmic stimulus can override and regulate them. This anticipates modern understanding of how rhythmic sensory input (rocking, music) activates the parasympathetic nervous system. The idea that external order can impose internal order connects to the Republic's theory of musical education shaping the soul (Republic 398c-402a). The theological dimension -- that the Bacchantes achieve 'sound mind' through divine help -- maintains the Laws' characteristic integration of psychology and religion.

narrative:
"Certainly."

"But if fear has such a power we ought to infer from these facts, that every soul which from youth upward has been familiar with fears, will be made more liable to fear, and every one will allow that this is the way to form a habit of cowardice and not of courage."

"No doubt."

"And, on the other hand, the habit of overcoming, from our youth upwards, the fears and terrors which beset us, may be said to be an exercise of courage."

"True."

"And we may say that the use of exercise and motion in the earliest years of life greatly contributes to create a part of virtue in the soul."

"Quite true."

"Further, a cheerful temper, or the reverse, may be regarded as having much to do with high spirit on the one hand, or with cowardice on the other."

"To be sure."

"Then now we must endeavour to show how and to what extent we may, if we please, without difficulty implant either character in the young."

"Certainly."

"There is a common opinion, that luxury makes the disposition of youth discontented and irascible and vehemently excited by trifles; that on the other hand excessive and savage servitude makes men mean and abject, and haters of their kind, and therefore makes them undesirable associates."

"But how must the state educate those who do not as yet understand the language of the country, and are therefore incapable of appreciating any sort of instruction?"

"I will tell you how: Every animal that is born is wont to utter some cry, and this is especially the case with man, and he is also affected with the inclination to weep more than any other animal."

"Quite true."
refashioned:
Cleinias agreed. The Athenian drew a further inference: if fear has such power, then every soul habituated to fears from youth will become more prone to fear. Everyone would agree this produces cowardice, not courage. Conversely, the habit of overcoming fears and terrors from youth onward is an exercise in courage. Early motion and exercise greatly contribute to developing virtue in the soul. Moreover, a cheerful disposition promotes spiritedness, while its opposite fosters cowardice.

They must therefore show how and to what extent they can implant the right character in the young. There is a common view that luxury makes youth discontented, irritable, and violently upset by trifles, while excessive and cruel servitude makes people mean, degraded, and hostile toward others -- unfit companions either way.

Cleinias posed a practical question: how should the state educate those who do not yet understand language and cannot comprehend instruction? The Athenian replied that every animal makes some cry at birth, but humans above all -- and humans are also more inclined to weeping than any other animal. Cleinias confirmed this.
elegant: If fear shapes the soul, then habitual exposure to fear breeds cowardice, while habitual overcoming of fear builds courage. Early motion and exercise cultivate virtue in the soul, and cheerfulness promotes spiritedness. Luxury makes youth irritable over trifles; excessive servitude makes them mean and misanthropic. But how do you educate those too young for language? Through the body: every animal cries at birth, but humans cry and weep more than any other creature.
summary: The Athenian argues that habitual fear breeds cowardice while overcoming fear builds courage, and that early childhood conditions -- neither luxurious nor harsh -- shape character. When Cleinias asks how to educate pre-verbal children, the Athenian turns to the body's natural expressions.
distilled: Early habits of overcoming fear build courage while luxury breeds irritability and harsh treatment breeds meanness, and pre-verbal children must be educated through physical rather than verbal means.
notes: Speakers: Cleinias and Athenian Stranger (dialogue). The discussion of courage as a habit formed in youth echoes Republic 429c-430b (courage as preserving right belief about what is fearful) and Aristotle's Nicomachean Ethics 2.1-3 (virtue as habit). The middle path between luxury and servitude parallels Aristotle's doctrine of the mean. The question about pre-verbal education is practically important and philosophically significant -- it pushes education theory beyond the merely intellectual. Compare Republic 401d-402a on music's pre-rational influence on the soul.

narrative: "Do not nurses, when they want to know what an infant desires, judge by these signs? -- when anything is brought to the infant and he is silent, then he is supposed to be pleased, but, when he weeps and cries out, then he is not pleased. For tears and cries are the inauspicious signs by which children show what they love and hate. Now the time which is thus spent is no less than three years, and is a very considerable portion of life to be passed ill or well."
refashioned: The Athenian pointed out that nurses determine what an infant wants by reading these very signs: when something is brought to the child and it remains silent, the child is pleased; when it weeps and cries out, it is displeased. Tears and cries are the unfavorable signals by which children reveal what they love and hate. This pre-verbal period lasts no less than three years -- a very considerable portion of life to be spent well or badly.
elegant: Nurses read infants' desires through silence (pleasure) and crying (displeasure). These first three pre-verbal years -- a substantial portion of life -- can be spent well or badly, and their quality matters enormously.
summary: Nurses interpret infant desires through silence (contentment) and crying (displeasure). The Athenian emphasizes that these first three pre-verbal years constitute a significant portion of life that profoundly affects development.
distilled: The first three pre-verbal years, read through an infant's silence and cries, are a crucial and substantial portion of life.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger (monologue). The observation about nurses reading infant signals is empirically grounded and connects to the broader Greek interest in semiotics (reading signs). The emphasis on the first three years as formative anticipates modern developmental psychology. The word 'inauspicious' (dyssema) carries religious overtones -- the child's cries are like ill omens, suggesting that unhappiness in infancy portends future problems. This reinforces the Athenian's argument that pre-verbal education through physical conditions is critically important.

narrative:
"True."

"Does not the discontented and ungracious nature appear to you to be full of lamentations and sorrows more than a good man ought to be?"

"Certainly."

"Well, but if during these three years every possible care were taken that our nursling should have as little of sorrow and fear, and in general of pain as was possible, might we not expect in early childhood to make his soul more gentle and cheerful?"

"To be sure, Stranger -- more especially if we could procure him a variety of pleasures."

"There I can no longer agree, Cleinias: you amaze me. To bring him up in such a way would be his utter ruin; for the beginning is always the most critical part of education. Let us see whether I am right."

"Proceed."
refashioned:
Cleinias agreed. The Athenian asked whether the discontented, ungracious person seems to be filled with more lamentation and sorrow than is fitting for a good person. Cleinias confirmed this. The Athenian then asked: if during these first three years, every care were taken to minimize sorrow, fear, and pain, would this not make the child's soul more gentle and cheerful?

Cleinias enthusiastically agreed -- especially, he added, if they could supply the child with a variety of pleasures. At this, the Athenian sharply disagreed: "You amaze me. Raising a child that way would be his utter ruin, for the beginning is always the most critical part of education." He asked Cleinias to hear him out, and Cleinias told him to proceed.
elegant: The discontented nature overflows with lamentation. If we minimize sorrow and fear in an infant's first three years, we might expect a gentler soul. But Cleinias goes too far in suggesting we maximize pleasures -- that would be the child's ruin. The beginning is the most critical part of education, and a surfeit of pleasure corrupts from the start.
summary: When Cleinias suggests maximizing an infant's pleasures to produce a cheerful soul, the Athenian sharply disagrees. While minimizing unnecessary pain is good, flooding a child with pleasures would be ruinous because the beginning of education is its most critical phase.
distilled: Minimizing unnecessary suffering in infancy is wise, but maximizing pleasures would ruin the child because the earliest phase of education is the most decisive.
notes: Speakers: Cleinias and Athenian Stranger (dialogue). This is a pivotal exchange where the Athenian corrects Cleinias' hedonistic instinct -- not all pleasure is beneficial, especially in education. This echoes the Republic's critique of undisciplined pleasure (Republic 559d-561a) and anticipates the Laws' own doctrine of the 'middle state' elaborated in 7.10. The principle that 'the beginning is the most critical part' appears also at Republic 377a-b regarding early exposure to stories. Aristotle quotes this principle approvingly in Politics 7.17.

narrative: "The point about which you and I differ is of great importance, and I hope that you, Megillus, will help to decide between us. For I maintain that the true life should neither seek for pleasures, nor, on the other hand, entirely avoid pains, but should embrace the middle state, which I just spoke of as gentle and benign, and is a state which we by some divine presage and inspiration rightly ascribe to God. Now, I say, he among men, too, who would be divine ought to pursue after this mean habit -- he should not rush headlong into pleasures, for he will not be free from pains; nor should we allow any one, young or old, male or female, to be thus given any more than ourselves, and least of all the newly-born infant, for in infancy more than at any other time the character is engrained by habit. Nay, more, if I were not afraid of appearing to be ridiculous, I would say that a woman during her year of pregnancy should of all women be most carefully tended, and kept from violent or excessive pleasures and pains, and should at that time cultivate gentleness and benevolence and kindness."
refashioned:
The Athenian declared that the point of disagreement between himself and Cleinias was of great importance, and he asked Megillus to help adjudicate. His position was this: the true life should neither chase after pleasures nor entirely avoid pains, but embrace the middle state -- gentle and benign -- which humans by divine intuition rightly attribute to God himself.

Anyone who aspires to the divine should pursue this balanced disposition. One should not rush headlong into pleasures, because pains are unavoidable regardless. This principle applies to everyone -- young and old, male and female -- but above all to the newborn infant, for in infancy character is shaped by habit more deeply than at any other time.

Moreover, if he were not afraid of seeming ridiculous, the Athenian would add that a pregnant woman should, of all women, be the most carefully attended to. She should be kept from violent or excessive pleasures and pains alike, and should cultivate gentleness, benevolence, and kindness throughout her pregnancy.
elegant: The true life neither chases pleasure nor flees pain, but embraces the middle state -- gentle and benign -- which we rightly attribute to God. Anyone aspiring to divinity should pursue this balance. It applies to all, but especially to newborns, whose characters are shaped by habit more than at any other age. Even pregnant women should be kept from extremes of pleasure and pain, cultivating gentleness and kindness instead.
summary: The Athenian argues for a middle path between pleasure-seeking and pain-avoidance, a gentle state he attributes to the divine nature. This balance is most critical for infants, whose character is formed by early habit, and should extend even to the care of pregnant women.
distilled: The ideal life pursues neither pleasure nor avoidance of pain but a gentle middle state attributed to God, and this balance is most crucial during infancy when character is formed by habit.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger (monologue, addressing both interlocutors). This passage articulates the Laws' distinctive ethical position: the 'middle state' (mese hexis) between pleasure and pain. This differs subtly from Aristotle's 'mean' (mesotes) in the Nicomachean Ethics, which concerns virtue between excess and deficiency. The attribution of this calm state to God connects to Laws 10's theology. The appeal to Megillus as arbiter is a dramatic device reflecting Sparta's cultural emphasis on moderation and discipline. The prenatal care recommendations again anticipate Aristotle's Politics 7.16.

narrative:
"You need not ask Megillus, Stranger, which of us has most truly spoken; for I myself agree that all men ought to avoid the life of unmingled pain or pleasure, and pursue always a middle course. And having spoken well, may I add that you have been well answered?"

"Very good, Cleinias; and now let us all three consider a further point."

"What is it?"
refashioned:
Cleinias told the Athenian there was no need to consult Megillus -- he himself agreed that everyone should avoid a life of pure pain or pure pleasure and pursue a middle course instead. He added, with some warmth, that having spoken well, the Athenian had also been well answered.

The Athenian accepted this graciously and proposed that all three of them now consider a further point. Cleinias asked what it was.
elegant: Cleinias concedes without needing Megillus as judge: everyone should avoid extremes of pain or pleasure and pursue the middle course. The Athenian proposes they now consider a further point together.
summary: Cleinias agrees with the Athenian's doctrine of the middle path between pleasure and pain without needing Megillus to adjudicate. The Athenian then moves to introduce a new topic.
distilled: Cleinias voluntarily accepts the doctrine of the middle path between pleasure and pain, and the discussion moves forward.
notes: Speakers: Cleinias and Athenian Stranger (dialogue). Cleinias' ready agreement here is dramatically significant -- it shows the Athenian's persuasive power. The phrase 'unmingled pain or pleasure' (akratos) uses wine-mixing vocabulary, a common metaphor in Plato (compare Philebus 61b-c on the 'mixed life'). Cleinias' slightly competitive remark about being 'well answered' adds human texture to the dialogue.

narrative:
"That all the matters which we are now describing are commonly called by the general name of unwritten customs, and what are termed the laws of our ancestors are all of similar nature. And the reflection which lately arose in our minds, that we can neither call these things laws, nor yet leave them unmentioned, is justified; for they are the bonds of the whole state, and come in between the written laws which are or are hereafter to be laid down; they are just ancestral customs of great antiquity, which, if they are rightly ordered and made habitual, shield and preserve the previously existing written law; but if they depart from right and fall into disorder, then they are like the props of builders which slip away out of their place and cause a universal ruin -- one part drags another down, and the fair superstructure falls because the old foundations are undermined. Reflecting upon this, Cleinias, you ought to bind together the new state in every possible way, omitting nothing, whether great or small, of what are called laws or manners or pursuits, for by these means a city is bound together, and all these things are only lasting when they depend upon one another; and, therefore, we must not wonder if we find that many apparently trifling customs or usages come pouring in and lengthening out our laws."

"Very true: we are disposed to agree with you."
refashioned:
The Athenian explained that everything they had been discussing falls under the general heading of "unwritten customs" -- what people call the laws of their ancestors. The earlier reflection -- that these things cannot properly be called laws yet cannot be left unmentioned -- was justified. These unwritten customs are the bonds of the entire state. They occupy the space between written laws already established and those yet to come. They are ancestral practices of great antiquity which, when rightly maintained and habitual, shield and preserve the written law. But when they fall into disorder, they are like a builder's props slipping out of place, causing the whole structure to collapse -- one part dragging another down, the fine superstructure falling because its old foundations have been undermined.

With this in mind, the Athenian urged Cleinias to bind the new state together in every possible way, omitting nothing -- whether great or small -- of what are called laws, customs, or practices. A city is held together by all of these, and they endure only when they depend on one another. They should not be surprised, therefore, if many seemingly trivial customs come pouring in and lengthening their laws.

Cleinias agreed they were disposed to accept this view.
elegant: What they have been discussing are "unwritten customs" -- ancestral practices that cannot be called laws yet cannot be ignored. These customs are the bonds of the state, occupying the space between written laws. When rightly maintained, they shield written law; when disordered, they are like slipping props that bring the whole structure down. A new state must be bound together by every means -- laws, customs, practices great and small -- for all depend on one another.
summary: The Athenian explains that unwritten customs are the bonds holding the state together, bridging the gaps between written laws. When maintained, they protect the legal order; when corrupted, they undermine it like collapsing foundations. The new state must attend to every custom, great and small.
distilled: Unwritten customs are the bonds of the state that either preserve or destroy the written law, and a well-founded city must attend to every practice, great and small.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger with Cleinias responding (dialogue). This is one of the most important theoretical passages in the Laws. The concept of 'unwritten laws' (agraphoi nomoi) was debated throughout Greek political thought -- Pericles invokes them in Thucydides 2.37, and they appear in Sophocles' Antigone (454-455). The building metaphor (props, foundations, superstructure) is vivid and architecturally precise. The insight that formal law depends on informal custom for its stability anticipates modern sociological and legal theory (e.g., Lon Fuller's 'internal morality of law'). Compare Republic 425a-e for a contrasting view where Socrates dismisses legislating minor customs.

narrative: "Up to the age of three years, whether of boy or girl, if a person strictly carries out our previous regulations and makes them a principal aim, he will do much for the advantage of the young creatures. But at three, four, five, and even six years the childish nature will require sports; now is the time to get rid of self-will in him, punishing him, but not so as to disgrace him. We were saying about slaves, that we ought neither to add insult to punishment so as to anger them, nor yet to leave them unpunished lest they become self-willed; and a like rule is to be observed in the case of the free-born. Children at that age have certain natural modes of amusement which they find out for themselves when they meet. And all the children who are between the ages of three and six ought to meet at the temples of the villages, the several families of a village uniting on one spot."
refashioned:
Up to age three, strictly following the previously outlined regulations will do a great deal for the welfare of the child, whether boy or girl. But from three to six years old, children's nature demands play. This is the time to curb self-will through punishment -- but never in a way that humiliates the child. The same principle was established for slaves: punishment should not include insult that provokes anger, nor should offenses go unpunished lest the slave become willful. The same rule applies to freeborn children.

At this age, children naturally invent their own forms of play when they come together. All children between three and six should gather at the village temples, with the families of each village assembling in one place.
elegant: Until age three, follow the earlier regulations carefully. From three to six, children need play, and self-will must be corrected through punishment that does not humiliate -- the same principle applied to slaves. Children naturally invent games when they gather, and all children aged three to six should meet together at village temples.
summary: Children up to three benefit from the earlier physical regimen. From three to six, play becomes essential, and self-will must be corrected without humiliation. Children of this age naturally create their own games and should gather together at village temples.
distilled: From ages three to six, children need communal play and correction of self-will through firm but non-humiliating discipline.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger (monologue). The parallel between disciplining children and disciplining slaves is striking and characteristic of the Laws' practical realism. The principle of punishment without humiliation is notably humane for its era. The communal gathering at temples establishes early socialization as a civic responsibility, not just a family matter. Compare Republic 537a on compulsion in education ('no forced learning can remain in the soul'). The village temple as gathering place shows the Laws' integration of religious and civic life.

narrative:
"The nurses are to see that the children behave properly and orderly -- they themselves and all their companies are to be under the control of twelve matrons, one for each company, who are annually selected to inspect them from the women previously mentioned, [i.e., the women who have authority over marriage], whom the guardians of the law appoint. These matrons shall be chosen by the women who have authority over marriage, one out of each tribe; all are to be of the same age; and let each of them, as soon as she is appointed, hold office and go to the temples every day, punishing all offenders, male or female, who are slaves or strangers, by the help of some of the public slaves; but if any citizen disputes the punishment, let her bring him before the wardens of the city; or, if there be no dispute, let her punish him herself.

"After the age of six years the time has arrived for the separation of the sexes -- let boys live with boys, and girls in like manner with girls. Now they must begin to learn -- the boys going to teachers of horsemanship and the use of the bow, the javelin, and sling, and the girls too, if they do not object, at any rate until they know how to manage these weapons, and especially how to handle heavy arms; for I may note, that the practice which now prevails is almost universally misunderstood."

"In what respect?"
refashioned:
The Athenian specified the administrative structure: nurses must ensure orderly behavior among the children. Both nurses and their groups shall be supervised by twelve matrons -- one per company -- selected annually from the women who oversee marriage, as appointed by the guardians of the law. One matron is chosen from each tribe, all of the same age. Each matron, upon appointment, must go to the temples daily, punishing offending slaves or foreigners with the help of public slaves. If a citizen disputes the punishment, the matron brings the case before the city wardens; if there is no dispute, she administers the punishment herself.

After age six, the sexes are to be separated -- boys living with boys, girls with girls. Formal education begins: boys learn horsemanship, archery, javelin-throwing, and slinging. Girls should learn the same skills, if they do not object -- at minimum until they can handle these weapons competently, especially heavy arms. The Athenian noted that the prevailing practice in this area is almost universally misunderstood.

Cleinias asked: in what respect?
elegant: Twelve matrons -- one per tribe, annually selected -- supervise the nurses and children, with authority to punish offenders. After age six, sexes separate and formal education begins: boys learn horsemanship, archery, javelin, and sling. Girls should learn the same, at least until they can handle heavy arms competently. Current practice is almost universally misguided on this point.
summary: The Athenian establishes a system of twelve elected matrons to oversee early childhood care. After age six, boys and girls separate and begin formal education in martial skills. Girls should also learn weapons handling, especially heavy arms -- a point the Athenian says is widely misunderstood.
distilled: After communal early childhood supervised by elected matrons, children at six begin formal martial education, with girls included in weapons training alongside boys.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias (dialogue). The institutional detail here is characteristic of the Laws' practical legislative ambition. The twelve matrons recall the Republic's female guardians (Republic 540c) but are more administratively specific. The inclusion of girls in martial education is radical for ancient Greece -- even Sparta, which trained women in athletics, did not go this far with weapons. The phrase 'if they do not object' shows the Athenian's awareness of cultural resistance. The 'misunderstanding' he mentions concerns handedness, discussed in the next passage (7.15).

narrative: "In that the right and left hand are supposed to be by nature differently suited for our various uses of them; whereas no difference is found in the use of the feet and the lower limbs; but in the use of the hands we are, as it were, maimed by the folly of nurses and mothers; for although our several limbs are by nature balanced, we create a difference in them by bad habit. In some cases this is of no consequence, as, for example, when we hold the lyre in the left hand, and the plectrum in the right, but it is downright folly to make the same distinction in other cases. The custom of the Scythians proves our error; for they not only hold the bow from them with the left hand and draw the arrow to them with their right, but use either hand for both purposes. And there are many similar examples in charioteering and other things, from which we may learn that those who make the left side weaker than the right act contrary to nature."
refashioned:
The Athenian explained the misunderstanding: people assume the right and left hands are naturally suited for different tasks. Yet no such distinction exists for the feet and lower limbs. When it comes to the hands, we are effectively crippled by the foolishness of nurses and mothers, because although our limbs are naturally balanced, we create asymmetry through bad habit.

In some cases this does not matter -- holding the lyre in the left hand and the plectrum in the right is fine. But applying this same distinction across all activities is sheer folly. The Scythians prove the point: they not only hold the bow with the left hand while drawing with the right, but use either hand interchangeably for both tasks. Many similar examples from chariot-driving and other skills show that those who make the left side weaker than the right are acting contrary to nature.
elegant: People wrongly assume the hands are naturally suited for different tasks. Our feet show no such bias -- the asymmetry in our hands is created by bad habit, not nature. In some cases the distinction is harmless, but in others it is folly. The Scythians prove this: they use either hand for the bow. Making the left side weaker than the right goes against nature.
summary: The Athenian argues that hand dominance is not natural but is created by habit -- nurses and mothers cripple children by training them to favor one hand. The Scythians, who use both hands equally for archery, prove that ambidexterity is the natural state.
distilled: Hand dominance is not natural but a product of bad habit; our limbs are inherently balanced, as the ambidextrous Scythians demonstrate.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger (monologue). This is a remarkably modern observation about handedness. While contemporary science shows some neurological basis for hand preference, the Athenian's emphasis on the role of training and habit in reinforcing asymmetry has merit. The Scythians, nomadic warriors of the Central Asian steppes, were famous for their mounted archery -- Herodotus describes their customs extensively (Histories 4.1-82). The argument from nature versus habit (physis vs. nomos) is a recurring theme in Greek philosophy, central to the Sophists and addressed in Protagoras 320c-328d.

narrative: "In the case of the plectrum, which is of horn only, and similar instruments, as I was saying, it is of no consequence, but makes a great difference, and may be of very great importance to the warrior who has to use iron weapons, bows and javelins, and the like; above all, when in heavy armour, he has to fight against heavy armour. And there is a very great difference between one who has learnt and one who has not, and between one who has been trained in gymnastic exercises and one who has not been. For as he who is perfectly skilled in the Pancratium or boxing or wrestling, is not unable to fight from his left side, and does not limp and draggle in confusion when his opponent makes him change his position, so in heavy-armed fighting, and in all other things if I am not mistaken, the like holds -- he who has these double powers of attack and defence ought not in any case to leave them either unused or untrained, if he can help; and if a person had the nature of Geryon or Briareus he ought to be able with his hundred hands to throw a hundred darts. Now, the magistrates, male and female, should see to all these things, the women superintending the nursing and amusements of the children, and the men superintending their education, that all of them, boys and girls alike, may be sound hand and foot, and may not, if they can help, spoil the gifts of nature by bad habits."
refashioned:
As the Athenian had noted, for the plectrum and similar instruments the distinction between hands is of no consequence. But it makes a great difference -- indeed, it may be critically important -- for a warrior using iron weapons, bows, javelins, and the like. This is especially true in heavy-armed combat against other heavy-armed fighters. The gap between the trained and the untrained is enormous. Just as a master of the Pancratium, boxing, or wrestling can fight equally well from either side and does not stumble in confusion when forced to change position, the same principle applies to heavy-armed combat and everything else. A person with these dual powers of attack and defense should never leave them unused or untrained. If someone had the hundred hands of Geryon or Briareus, he should be able to throw a hundred darts.

Magistrates, both male and female, should oversee all of this -- women superintending the nursing and play of young children, men superintending their education -- so that all children, boys and girls alike, may be sound in hand and foot and not ruin nature's gifts through bad habits.
elegant: For instruments the hand distinction hardly matters, but for warriors with iron weapons and heavy armor, ambidexterity may be decisive. A skilled boxer or wrestler fights equally from either side without confusion -- the same applies to armed combat. Anyone with dual powers of attack should train them fully. Male and female magistrates should ensure all children develop both sides equally, not spoiling nature's gifts through bad habits.
summary: The Athenian argues that while hand preference is harmless in music, it is critical in combat -- a trained fighter must be equally capable from either side. Magistrates should ensure all children, boys and girls, are trained ambidextrously to preserve nature's balanced gifts.
distilled: Ambidextrous training is essential for warriors since a skilled fighter must be able to attack and defend equally from either side.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger (monologue). Geryon was the three-bodied giant killed by Heracles; Briareus (Aegaeon) was one of the Hundred-Handed Ones (Hecatoncheires) in Hesiod's Theogony. The Pancratium was a combined boxing-wrestling event in the ancient Olympics, considered the most demanding athletic contest. The practical military argument for ambidexterity is sound -- warriors who can only fight from one side are vulnerable. The joint oversight by male and female magistrates again reflects the Laws' progressive inclusion of women in civic governance, developing the principles of Republic 451c-457b.

narrative:
"Education has two branches -- one of gymnastic, which is concerned with the body, and the other of music, which is designed for the improvement of the soul. And gymnastic has also two branches -- dancing and wrestling; and one sort of dancing imitates musical recitation, and aims at preserving dignity and freedom, the other aims at producing health, agility, and beauty in the limbs and parts of the body, giving the proper flexion and extension to each of them, a harmonious motion being diffused everywhere, and forming a suitable accompaniment to the dance.

"As regards wrestling, the tricks which Antaeus and Cercyon devised in their systems out of a vain spirit of competition, or the tricks of boxing which Epeius or Amycus invented, are useless and unsuitable for war, and do not deserve to have much said about them; but the art of wrestling erect and keeping free the neck and hands and sides, working with energy and constancy, with a composed strength, and for the sake of health -- these are always useful, and are not to be neglected, but to be enjoined alike on masters and scholars, when we reach that part of legislation; and we will desire the one to give their instructions freely, and the others to receive them thankfully."
refashioned:
The Athenian outlined the structure of education: it has two branches. Gymnastic is concerned with the body, and music with the improvement of the soul. Gymnastic itself divides into two: dancing and wrestling.

Dancing has two forms. One imitates musical recitation, aiming to preserve dignity and freedom. The other aims at health, agility, and beauty of the body, giving each limb its proper flexion and extension, diffusing harmonious motion throughout, and forming a fitting accompaniment to the dance.

As for wrestling, the Athenian dismissed the tricks devised by Antaeus and Cercyon out of competitive vanity, and the boxing techniques invented by Epeius and Amycus, as useless for war and not worth discussing. But the art of upright wrestling -- keeping the neck, hands, and sides free, working with energy, constancy, and composed strength, for the sake of health -- is always useful. This form should be required of both teachers and students when the time comes for that part of legislation, with teachers giving instruction generously and students receiving it gratefully.
elegant: Education has two branches: gymnastic for the body, music for the soul. Gymnastic divides into dancing and wrestling. One type of dance preserves dignity through imitating song; the other develops health, agility, and bodily beauty. Flashy wrestling tricks invented by Antaeus, Cercyon, Epeius, and Amycus are useless for war. But upright wrestling -- keeping neck, hands, and sides free, with composed strength for health's sake -- is always valuable and should be required of all.
summary: The Athenian divides education into gymnastic (body) and music (soul), and gymnastic into dancing and wrestling. He rejects flashy wrestling tricks as useless for war but endorses upright wrestling for health and military readiness.
distilled: Education divides into gymnastic for the body and music for the soul, with useful wrestling and dignified dancing preferred over flashy competitive tricks.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger (monologue). The gymnastic/music division is the same as Republic 376e. Antaeus was the Libyan giant wrestler defeated by Heracles; Cercyon was the Eleusinian wrestling king killed by Theseus. Epeius was the Greek boxer at Troy who also built the Trojan Horse (Iliad 23.664-699). Amycus was the Bebrycian king who forced travelers to box him, defeated by Polydeuces (Apollonius Rhodius, Argonautica 2.1-97). The preference for upright wrestling over ground-fighting reflects practical military priorities -- a soldier in armor needs to stay on his feet.

narrative: "Nor, again, must we omit suitable imitations of war in our choruses; here in Crete you have the armed dances of the Curetes, and the Lacedaemonians have those of the Dioscuri. And our virgin lady, delighting in the amusement of the dance, thought it not fit to amuse herself with empty hands; she must be clothed in a complete suit of armour, and in this attire go through the dance; and youths and maidens should in every respect imitate her, esteeming highly the favour of the Goddess, both with a view to the necessities of war, and to festive occasions: it will be right also for the boys, until such time as they go out to war, to make processions and supplications to all the Gods in goodly array, armed and on horseback, in dances, and marches, fast or slow, offering up prayers to the Gods and to the sons of Gods; and also engaging in contests and preludes of contests, if at all, with these objects: For these sorts of exercises, and no others, are useful both in peace and war, and are beneficial alike to states and to private houses."
refashioned:
The Athenian continued: they must not neglect suitable imitations of war within their choral performances. Crete already has the armed dances of the Curetes, and the Lacedaemonians have those of the Dioscuri. Athena herself -- their virgin goddess -- delighted in dance but refused to dance with empty hands. She clothed herself in full armor to perform her dance. Youths and maidens should imitate her in every way, honoring the goddess both for the necessities of war and for festive celebrations.

Boys, until they go to war, should make processions and supplications to all the gods in splendid array -- armed and on horseback, in dances and marches both fast and slow -- offering prayers to the gods and sons of gods. They should engage in contests and preliminary contests with these same purposes in mind. These exercises, and only these, are useful in both peace and war, and beneficial alike to states and to private households.
elegant: Choruses should include armed dances imitating war -- as the Curetes dance in Crete and the Dioscuri in Sparta. Athena herself danced in full armor, and youths should imitate her. Boys should process armed and on horseback, praying to the gods through dance, march, and contest. Only exercises serving both war and worship are truly useful to states and households.
summary: Armed dances should be part of civic choruses, following the example of Cretan Curetes, Spartan Dioscuri, and Athena herself who danced in full armor. Boys should regularly practice armed processions, marches, and contests as preparation for both war and worship.
distilled: Armed dances and martial processions, following the divine example of Athena dancing in armor, serve both military preparation and religious worship.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger (monologue). The Curetes were mythical warriors who danced around the infant Zeus to protect him from Kronos -- their armed dance (pyrrhiche) was central to Cretan religious practice. The Dioscuri (Castor and Pollux) were Spartan patron deities. The 'virgin lady' is Athena, whose armed birth and warlike character are central to Athenian identity. The image of Athena dancing in armor may reference the Panathenaic festival. The integration of military training, religious ritual, and aesthetic performance is characteristic of the Laws' holistic approach to education. Compare Republic 399a-b on appropriate musical modes for warriors.

narrative:
"But other labours and sports and exercises of the body are unworthy of freemen, O Megillus and Cleinias."

"I have now completely described the kind of gymnastic which I said at first ought to be described; if you know of any better, will you communicate your thoughts?"

"It is not easy, Stranger, to put aside these principles of gymnastic and wrestling and to enunciate better ones."

"Now we must say what has yet to be said about the gifts of the Muses and of Apollo: before, we fancied that we had said all, and that gymnastic alone remained; but now we see clearly what points have been omitted, and should be first proclaimed; of these, then, let us proceed to speak."

"By all means."

"Let me tell you once more -- although you have heard me say the same before -- that caution must be always exercised, both by the speaker and by the hearer, about anything that is very singular and unusual. For my tale is one which many a man would be afraid to tell, and yet I have a confidence which makes me go on."

"What have you to say, Stranger?"
refashioned:
The Athenian declared that other forms of physical labor, sport, and exercise are unworthy of free citizens. He stated that he had now fully described the kind of gymnastic he had promised to discuss, and invited Megillus and Cleinias to propose anything better.

Cleinias admitted it was not easy to set aside these principles and articulate superior ones.

The Athenian then shifted focus: they must now address what remains to be said about the gifts of the Muses and Apollo. Earlier, they thought they had covered everything and only gymnastic remained, but now they could see important points had been omitted that should be proclaimed first.

Cleinias urged him to continue. The Athenian cautioned that both speaker and listener must exercise care when the subject is singular and unusual. His argument was one that many would fear to make, yet he had the confidence to press forward.

Cleinias asked what he had to say.
elegant: Other sports and exercises are beneath free citizens. The gymnastic program is now complete. But important matters concerning the Muses and Apollo have been omitted and must be addressed first. Caution is required -- what follows is unusual, and many would be afraid to say it -- but the Athenian presses on with confidence.
summary: Having completed his account of gymnastic, the Athenian turns to the still-unaddressed topic of musical education. He warns that what he is about to say is singular and unusual -- something many would be afraid to assert -- but he has the confidence to proceed.
distilled: With gymnastic fully described, the Athenian turns to musical education with a warning that his next argument will be bold and unusual.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias (dialogue, Megillus also addressed). The dismissal of certain sports as 'unworthy of freemen' reflects aristocratic Greek values about leisure (schole) and its proper uses. The transition to 'the gifts of the Muses and Apollo' signals the move from physical to cultural education. The Athenian's cautionary preface about 'singular and unusual' claims builds dramatic tension for his argument about children's play (7.20), which he considers one of his most important and counter-intuitive points.

narrative:
"I say that in states generally no one has observed that the plays of childhood have a great deal to do with the permanence or want of permanence in legislation. For when plays are ordered with a view to children having the same plays, and amusing themselves after the same manner, and finding delight in the same playthings, the more solemn institutions of the state are allowed to remain undisturbed. Whereas if sports are disturbed, and innovations are made in them, and they constantly change, and the young never speak of their having the same likings, or the same established notions of good and bad taste, either in the bearing of their bodies or in their dress, but he who devises something new and out of the way in figures and colours and the like is held in special honour, we may truly say that no greater evil can happen in a state; for he who changes the sports is secretly changing the manners of the young, and making the old to be dishonoured among them and the new to be honoured. And I affirm that there is nothing which is a greater injury to all states than saying or thinking thus. Will you hear me tell how great I deem the evil to be?"

"You mean the evil of blaming antiquity in states?"

"Exactly."

"If you are speaking of that, you will find in us hearers who are disposed to receive what you say not unfavourably but most favourably."

"I should expect so."

"Proceed."
refashioned:
The Athenian made his bold claim: in states generally, no one has noticed that children's play has a profound connection to the stability of legislation. When children's games remain constant -- when children play the same way, enjoy the same amusements, and delight in the same toys -- the more serious institutions of the state remain undisturbed. But when games are disrupted, when innovations are constantly introduced, when the young never share the same tastes or the same established sense of good and bad -- whether in how they carry themselves or how they dress -- and when whoever invents something novel in shapes, colors, and the like receives special honor, then no greater evil can befall a state. The person who changes children's games is secretly changing the character of the young, making the old ways despised and the new ones honored. Nothing, the Athenian affirmed, causes greater harm to states than this attitude.

Cleinias guessed he meant the evil of disparaging tradition. Exactly, the Athenian confirmed. Cleinias assured him that on this subject, being Cretans and Spartans, they would be the most receptive audience imaginable. The Athenian expected as much and asked to continue.
elegant: No one has noticed that children's play profoundly affects the stability of legislation. When games remain constant, institutions remain stable. When games constantly change and novelty is celebrated, the character of youth is secretly altered -- old ways become despised, new ways honored. No greater evil can befall a state than this habit of disparaging tradition in favor of innovation.
summary: The Athenian argues that constant innovation in children's games secretly undermines the stability of the entire state by reshaping young people's character and teaching them to despise tradition. Stable play produces stable institutions; changing play produces changing values.
distilled: Innovation in children's games secretly undermines civic stability by teaching the young to despise established customs and honor novelty.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger with Cleinias responding (dialogue). This is one of the Laws' most famous and controversial arguments -- that children's play shapes political stability. The conservatism here goes beyond the Republic's censorship of poetry (Republic 376e-398b) to target even changes in children's games. Cleinias and Megillus, as representatives of traditionally conservative Crete and Sparta, are the ideal audience. The argument anticipates modern observations about cultural change beginning with shifts in popular taste. The Egyptian model of cultural fixity, mentioned in 7.23, is offered as the positive example.

narrative:
"Well, then, let us give all the greater heed to one another's words. The argument affirms that any change whatever except from evil is the most dangerous of all things; this is true in the case of the seasons and of the winds, in the management of our bodies and the habits of our minds -- true of all things except, as I said before, of the bad. He who looks at the constitution of individuals accustomed to eat any sort of meat, or drink any drink, or to do any work which they can get, may see that they are at first disordered by them, but afterwards, as time goes on, their bodies grow adapted to them, and they learn to know and like variety, and have good health and enjoyment of life; and if ever afterwards they are confined again to a superior diet, at first they are troubled with disorders, and with difficulty become habituated to their new food.

"A similar principle we may imagine to hold good about the minds of men and the natures of their souls. For when they have been brought up in certain laws, which by some Divine Providence have remained unchanged during long ages, so that no one has any memory or tradition of their ever having been otherwise than they are, then every one is afraid and ashamed to change that which is established. The legislator must somehow find a way of implanting this reverence for antiquity, and I would propose the following way: People are apt to fancy, as I was saying before, that when the plays of children are altered they are merely plays, not seeing that the most serious and detrimental consequences arise out of the change; and they readily comply with the child's wishes instead of deterring him, not considering that these children who make innovations in their games, when they grow up to be men, will be different from the last generation of children, and, being different, will desire a different sort of life, and under the influence of this desire will want other institutions and laws; and no one of them reflects that there will follow what I just now called the greatest of evils to states. Changes in bodily fashions are no such serious evils, but frequent changes in the praise and censure of manners are the greatest of evils, and require the utmost prevision."
refashioned:
The Athenian urged close attention: their argument was that any change, except away from evil, is the most dangerous of all things. This holds for the seasons and winds, for bodily health and mental habits -- for everything except what is already bad. Consider people accustomed to eating any food, drinking any drink, doing any available work: at first their bodies are disordered, but over time they adapt, learn to enjoy variety, and maintain good health. If they are later confined to a supposedly superior diet, they initially suffer and struggle to adjust.

The same principle applies to minds and souls. When people have been raised under laws that, by some divine providence, have remained unchanged for ages -- so long that no one remembers them ever being different -- then everyone feels fear and shame at the thought of changing what is established. The legislator must find a way to implant this reverence for tradition.

The Athenian proposed this approach: people tend to think that changes in children's games are trivial -- mere play. They do not see the serious consequences. They yield to children's wishes instead of holding firm, not realizing that children who innovate in their games will grow into adults different from the previous generation. Being different, they will want different lives, different institutions, different laws. No one reflects that this leads to the greatest evil in states. Changes in bodily fashion are not so serious, but frequent changes in what society praises and blames regarding conduct are the gravest of evils, requiring the utmost foresight.
elegant: Any change except away from evil is dangerous -- in seasons, bodily health, and mental habits. Bodies adapt to consistent conditions; disruption causes disorder even when the change seems beneficial. The same applies to souls raised under stable laws: long custom creates reverence that makes change feel shameful. Parents who indulge children's desire for novelty in play do not realize these children will grow into adults demanding new institutions and laws. Changes in fashion are trivial; changes in moral standards are the greatest evil.
summary: The Athenian argues that all change except from evil is dangerous, drawing parallels between bodily adaptation and cultural stability. Laws unchanged for ages create natural reverence; children's play innovations, though seemingly trivial, produce adults who demand different institutions. Changes in moral standards are the gravest evil of all.
distilled: All change except from evil is dangerous because stability creates reverence, and children who innovate in play grow into adults who demand new institutions and new moral standards.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger (monologue). This passage extends the argument from 7.20 with a biological analogy -- the body adapts to consistent conditions and is disturbed by change, even beneficial change. This echoes Hippocratic medical thinking. The claim that 'any change except from evil is dangerous' is the Laws' most explicitly conservative thesis, going well beyond the Republic's more nuanced view. The distinction between changes in bodily fashion (superficial) and changes in moral praise and censure (fundamental) is analytically sharp. The 'Divine Providence' that preserves laws connects to the Laws' theological framework developed in Book 10.

narrative:
"To be sure."

"And now do we still hold to our former assertion, that rhythms and music in general are imitations of good and evil characters in men? What say you?"

"That is the only doctrine which we can admit."

"Must we not, then, try in every possible way to prevent our youth from even desiring to imitate new modes either in dance or song? nor must any one be allowed to offer them varieties of pleasures."

"Most true."

"Can any of us imagine a better mode of effecting this object than that of the Egyptians?"

"What is their method?"
refashioned:
Cleinias agreed. The Athenian asked whether they still held to their earlier assertion that rhythms and music in general are imitations of good and evil characters in people. Cleinias replied that this was the only doctrine they could accept.

Then, the Athenian pressed, must they not try every possible means to prevent their youth from even wanting to imitate new modes of dance or song? No one should be permitted to offer them novel varieties of pleasure.

Cleinias emphatically agreed. The Athenian then asked: could any of them imagine a better way to achieve this than the method of the Egyptians? Cleinias asked what that method was.
elegant: Music and rhythm imitate good and evil character -- this doctrine stands. Therefore youth must be prevented from even desiring new modes of dance or song, and no one should offer them novel pleasures. The best model for achieving this is Egypt.
summary: Reaffirming that music imitates moral character, the Athenian argues youth must be prevented from desiring new musical modes. He introduces Egypt as the model for achieving cultural fixity in the arts.
distilled: Since music imitates moral character, youth must be kept from desiring novel modes, following the Egyptian model of artistic fixity.
notes: Speakers: Cleinias and Athenian Stranger (dialogue). The doctrine that music imitates character comes from Republic 398c-401d and Laws 2.654a-660a. The reference to Egypt as a model of cultural stability is developed in the next passage (7.23). The Athenian's admiration for Egypt's artistic conservatism is historically grounded -- Egyptian art maintained remarkably consistent forms over millennia. This passage connects the abstract argument about change (7.21) to concrete policy about musical regulation.

narrative:
"To consecrate every sort of dance or melody. First we should ordain festivals -- calculating for the year what they ought to be, and at what time, and in honour of what Gods, sons of Gods, and heroes they ought to be celebrated; and, in the next place, what hymns ought to be sung at the several sacrifices, and with what dances the particular festival is to be honoured. This has to be arranged at first by certain persons, and, when arranged, the whole assembly of the citizens are to offer sacrifices and libations to the Fates and all the other Gods, and to consecrate the several odes to gods and heroes: and if any one offers any other hymns or dances to any one of the Gods, the priests and priestesses, acting in concert with the guardians of the law, shall, with the sanction of religion and the law, exclude him, and he who is excluded, if he do not submit, shall be liable all his life long to have a suit of impiety brought against him by any one who likes."

"Very good."

"In the consideration of this subject, let us remember what is due to ourselves."

"To what are you referring?"
refashioned:
The Athenian described the Egyptian method: consecrate every form of dance and melody. First, ordain the year's festivals -- determining what they should be, when they should occur, and which gods, demigods, and heroes they should honor. Next, determine what hymns should be sung at each sacrifice and what dances should accompany each festival. This must initially be arranged by designated authorities. Once established, the entire citizen body offers sacrifices and libations to the Fates and all the other gods, consecrating the official odes to their respective gods and heroes.

If anyone offers unauthorized hymns or dances to any god, the priests and priestesses, acting together with the guardians of the law, shall exclude that person with the sanction of both religion and law. If the excluded person refuses to submit, anyone may bring a suit of impiety against him for the rest of his life.

Cleinias approved. The Athenian added a caveat: in considering this subject, they must remember what is due to themselves. Cleinias asked what he meant.
